content.text: What it is

Hypercare exit criteria are the measurable, pre-agreed conditions that must all be true before a launch team formally closes the heightened-attention hypercare period and transitions to steady-state operations. A transition plan is the accompanying document that specifies what changes -- alert thresholds, on-call staffing levels, SLA response times, reporting cadence -- when exit criteria are met.

Without explicit exit criteria, hypercare periods either end too early (the team declares victory before the system has demonstrated sustained stability) or drag on indefinitely (the team never feels confident enough to reduce intensity, burning out the on-call staff). Both failures are common. The exit criteria document converts a subjective judgment call into an objective checklist with stakeholder sign-off.

For agentic AI systems, exit criteria must include AI-specific signals that traditional software does not require: model output quality metrics, prompt injection detection baseline, tool-call success rates, and LLM provider reliability data. A system that has zero infrastructure incidents but degrades silently in output quality has not met its hypercare exit criteria.
Why it matters

Hypercare is expensive. The additional on-call staffing, daily stakeholder reviews, tighter alert thresholds that generate more noise, and engineering bandwidth devoted to monitoring rather than feature development represent a real cost. That cost is justified immediately post-launch, but continuing indefinitely is not sustainable.

At the same time, premature exit from hypercare exposes customers to elevated risk before the team has built the operational confidence and tooling to respond at steady-state speed. The exit criteria document resolves this tension by making the exit decision data-driven rather than political or calendar-driven.

The transition plan is equally important. If the team exits hypercare but does not formally update alert thresholds, SLAs, and on-call rosters, the operational model becomes ambiguous. Ambiguity in operations causes delayed responses and inconsistent behavior during incidents.
Benefits
Objective exit decision: all stakeholders agree on the criteria before launch, removing negotiation under pressure when the team is eager to scale back intensity.
Prevents premature exit: a team that 'feels' ready but has not met stability thresholds cannot declare exit without a criteria breach visible to everyone.
Prevents indefinite extension: if criteria are met, the team is obligated to exit, freeing engineering capacity for future work.
Forces operational documentation: completing the transition checklist ensures runbooks, dashboards, and on-call rosters are ready for steady-state before hypercare ends.
Stakeholder alignment: product, engineering, and customer success all sign off on the same criteria, preventing post-exit complaints.
Trade-offs
Criteria can become a checkbox exercise: teams under time pressure may set exit criteria that are trivially met. Criteria must be genuinely meaningful.
Multiple gate failures create dependency chains that make it hard to identify the critical path to exit.
Rigid criteria miss context: a single P0 incident near end of hypercare that is fully resolved may technically reset the stability clock unnecessarily.
Quality metrics are hard to automate: for agentic AI, output quality criteria require a human review pipeline or separate evaluation system built before launch.
How it works
Exit criteria are organized into three gates evaluated weekly. All three gates must pass in the same evaluation cycle for hypercare to end.

Gate 1, Stability, measures whether the service has demonstrated the reliability it was designed to deliver. Typical metrics: SLO met for 7 consecutive days, zero P0 incidents in the last 14 days, fewer than one P1 per week, error budget consumption below 40% for the most recent 30-day window. For agentic AI, add: task completion rate above SLO threshold, model latency within 1.5x of P50 baseline, zero prompt injection incidents.

Gate 2, Operational Readiness, measures whether the team can sustain operations without the hypercare support structure. Items: all incident runbooks reviewed and tested, steady-state on-call rotation staffed, all open P2/P3 incidents assigned to sprints, monitoring dashboards verified by SRE lead.

Gate 3, Stakeholder Sign-Off, ensures that customer success, product, security, and compliance have reviewed stability data and approved transition. This prevents engineering from unilaterally declaring victory while customers are still experiencing issues.
If any gate fails, hypercare extends by one week and the gate is re-evaluated. The weekly cadence prevents both daily re-evaluation anxiety and monthly drift.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Hypercare exit decision gate flow and steady-state transition checklist

content.text: The three gates must all pass in the same weekly evaluation cycle. The transition checklist documents every operational parameter that changes when hypercare ends.
When to use it

Explicitly written exit criteria are required for any production launch where a hypercare window has been formally declared. This includes initial product launches, major version upgrades that change agent behavior, and migrations to a new LLM provider or model version.

Exit criteria are not needed for routine deployments that do not change the agent's core behavior. But for any launch where the team has increased on-call staffing and set tighter alert thresholds, the exit criteria document is the mechanism for reversing those changes in a controlled, agreed way.
How to implement
Draft exit criteria two weeks before launch while the team still has distance from launch pressure to set meaningful thresholds.
Include AI-specific stability metrics: task completion rate, model latency P95 and P99, tool-call success rate, output quality score if measurable, and safety/guardrail metrics.
Assign gate owners: Gate 1 (SRE lead), Gate 2 (engineering manager), Gate 3 (product manager).
Schedule weekly exit criteria reviews from day 7 post-launch. Keep reviews under 30 minutes.
Document the transition delta: for every operational parameter tighter during hypercare, write down what it changes to after exit.
Build the transition memo template: a one-page document the IC sends when exit is declared.
Define an exception process for near-miss gate failures requiring unanimous stakeholder agreement (documented).
Schedule a 30-day retrospective after exit to assess whether steady-state operations are working as expected.
Examples
A concrete exit criteria document for an agentic AI travel booking system:

# Hypercare Exit Criteria -- TravelBot AI v2.0

## Gate 1: Stability

| Criterion | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Task completion rate (30d) | >= 99.2% | PASS |
| P0 incidents in last 14 days | 0 | PASS |
| P1 incidents in last 7 days | <= 1 | PASS |
| Error budget consumed (30d) | <= 40% | PASS |
| Model latency P95 | <= 4.5s | PASS |
| Tool-call success rate | >= 98.5% | PASS |

Gate 1 Status: PASS

## Gate 2: Operational Readiness
- [x] All P2/P3 incidents triaged and assigned to sprint backlog
- [x] Runbooks reviewed and tested in game day
- [x] Steady-state on-call rotation filled (6 engineers)
- [x] Monitoring dashboards reviewed and approved by SRE lead

Gate 2 Status: PASS

## Gate 3: Stakeholder Sign-Off
- [x] Customer Success: approved
- [x] Product Owner: approved
- [x] Security: approved (no outstanding findings)
- [x] Compliance: approved

Gate 3 Status: PASS

## EXIT DECISION: APPROVED
Hypercare ends: 2024-07-16T00:00 UTC
A Python script that automates Gate 1 evaluation by querying Prometheus:

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

PROMETHEUS_URL = "http://prometheus.internal:9090"

@dataclass
class GateCriteria:
    name: str
    target: float
    value: float
    passes: bool = field(init=False)

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.passes = self.value >= self.target

def query_instant(promql: str) -> float:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{PROMETHEUS_URL}/api/v1/query",
        params={"query": promql}
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    result = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    return float(result[0]["value"][1]) if result else 0.0

def evaluate_gate1(service: str = "travel-agent", slo: float = 0.992) -> List[GateCriteria]:
    task_rate = query_instant(
        f'sum(rate(agent_tasks_completed_total{{service="{service}"}}[30d]))'
        f' / sum(rate(agent_tasks_total{{service="{service}"}}[30d]))'
    )
    p0_count = query_instant(
        f'count_over_time(ALERTS{{alertname="P0Incident",service="{service}",alertstate="firing"}}[14d])'
    )
    budget_remaining = query_instant(
        f'(sum(rate(agent_tasks_completed_total{{service="{service}"}}[30d]))'
        f' / sum(rate(agent_tasks_total{{service="{service}"}}[30d])) - {slo}) / (1 - {slo})'
    )
    return [
        GateCriteria("Task completion rate (30d)", slo, task_rate),
        GateCriteria("P0 incidents (14d) -- passes when 0", 0, -p0_count),
        GateCriteria("Error budget remaining", 0.6, budget_remaining),
    ]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    criteria = evaluate_gate1()
    all_pass = all(c.passes for c in criteria)
    for c in criteria:
        status = "PASS" if c.passes else "FAIL"
        print(f"  [{status}] {c.name}: {c.value:.3f} (target: {c.target:.3f})")
    print(f"Gate 1: {'PASS' if all_pass else 'FAIL'}")
Real-world example

A B2B SaaS company launched an agentic AI contract review system with a 4-week hypercare window. During week 2, a P1 incident (incorrect clause extraction on a specific contract format) failed Gate 1. Rather than pressuring the team to waive criteria, the gate failure forced deployment of a format-specific fix that improved overall extraction accuracy. Week 3 Gate 1 passed. Week 4 all three gates passed.

The transition memo at exit documented four specific changes: alert thresholds relaxed by 30%, on-call rotation expanded from 4 to 8 engineers on weekly rotations, P0 response SLA extended from 1 hour to 2 hours, and daily error budget review moved to weekly. Because these changes were pre-documented, no engineer was surprised or had to make judgment calls about what 'steady-state' meant.

Three months later, the team noted that two of the stability gate metrics -- model latency P95 and tool-call success rate -- were the same metrics that caught two subsequent incidents before they became P0 events. The hypercare exit criteria had become the team's core reliability health indicators.
Common mistakes
Setting exit criteria after the hypercare period starts: criteria set under time pressure to exit are almost always too easy. Write them at launch planning.
Using only infrastructure metrics: a service that passes availability and latency criteria but has degraded LLM output quality has not met exit criteria for an agentic AI system.
No stakeholder sign-off gate: engineering-only exit decisions are often contested by customer success or product months later.
No exception process: a rigid criterion that cannot be waived for a fully-understood, fully-mitigated incident forces meaningless calendar-waiting.
Forgetting the transition delta document: teams that change on-call rosters and alert thresholds informally create ambiguity during the next incident.
Not scheduling a post-exit retrospective: the 30-day retrospective validates whether steady-state operations are actually working as predicted.
Best practices
Co-author exit criteria with all stakeholders before launch day, not during hypercare.
Tie criteria directly to the SLOs committed to customers: exit criteria detached from customer commitments are meaningless.
Include a 7-consecutive-day stability requirement rather than a single-point-in-time check.
Build the evaluation into a weekly automated report so gate owners can certify with data, not memory.
Run the exit criteria evaluation as a formal meeting with minutes; do not let exit happen via Slack agreement.
Archive the signed exit criteria document alongside the post-launch retrospective as part of the system's operational history.
Performance and scalability

Exit criteria evaluation must be automated to be trustworthy. For high-volume agentic AI services processing millions of tasks per day, manually computing task completion rate or error budget consumption is error-prone. Build Prometheus recording rules or Datadog monitors that continuously track Gate 1 metrics so the weekly review is a dashboard check, not a manual data pull.
For multi-region deployments, exit criteria must be evaluated per region and globally. A system that meets stability criteria in one region but not another has not met global exit criteria.
Tooling and ecosystem
Jira or Linear: track each exit criterion as a ticket with owner and pass/fail status updated weekly.
Confluence or Notion: host the exit criteria document and transition plan with version history and stakeholder approval signatures.
Prometheus + Grafana: automate Gate 1 metric collection and build a dedicated exit criteria dashboard.
PagerDuty or OpsGenie: generate the on-call rotation metrics needed for Gate 2 verification.
Slack: post weekly exit criteria status updates to a dedicated channel so stakeholders can see progress without attending the review meeting.
Comparison with alternatives

Calendar-based exit (hypercare ends on day 28 regardless): the most common approach because it requires no criteria definition work. It fails because the calendar does not know whether the system is stable. Teams using calendar-based exit frequently return to hypercare intensity informally after steady-state problems emerge.

Continuous hypercare (never formally exit): some teams avoid the exit conversation by maintaining hypercare-level intensity indefinitely. This burns out the on-call team within months and is not sustainable.
Maturity model-based exit (five-level maturity assessment): more rigorous but much heavier to execute. Appropriate for mission-critical regulated systems but overkill for most agentic AI deployments.
Security and reliability

The security sign-off in Gate 3 must specifically verify that any post-launch security findings -- prompt injection attempts, data leakage from tool outputs, unauthorized API access -- have been triaged and either mitigated or accepted as known risk. Do not exit hypercare with open Severity 1 or 2 security findings.

Reliability sign-off should include verification that the system has been tested against its disaster recovery plan. An untested DR runbook provides false confidence regardless of how well-written it is.
Mental model

Think of hypercare exit criteria as the preflight checklist before takeoff -- except you write it while the plane is still on the ground, before the pressure to fly. Once the criteria are written, the decision to take off is mechanical: check the list, not your gut. This discipline is what separates reliable operations from heroic operations.

The transition plan is the instrument panel reconfiguration that happens after reaching cruising altitude. Steady-state operations use different alert thresholds, staffing levels, and review cadences than hypercare -- and those differences must be documented and applied deliberately.
Key takeaways
Exit criteria must be written before launch day with stakeholder sign-off, not after the team decides it wants to exit.
Three gates cover the dimensions that matter: system stability, team operational readiness, and stakeholder alignment.
AI-specific metrics (task completion rate, model latency, output quality, tool-call success) must appear in Gate 1 alongside infrastructure SLOs.
A transition plan documents every operational parameter that changes at exit; without it, the team operates in ambiguity during the first post-hypercare incident.
Calendar-based exit is a trap; only data-based exit criteria prevent premature exit and indefinite hypercare drift.
